audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Joshua / Yehoshua east- and west-Jordan defeated-kings list, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for Sihon and Og territories, Moses/Joshua transition, western geography, thirty-one named kings, duplicated campaign memory, and list rhetoric that compresses peoples into conquered rulers.

verses.restored: These are kings of land whom children of Israel struck and whose land they possessed across Jordan toward sunrise, from wadi Arnon to Mount Hermon and all Arabah eastward:
Sihon king of Amorite, dwelling in Heshbon, ruling from Aroer on edge of wadi Arnon and middle of wadi, and half Gilead as far as wadi Jabbok, border of sons of Ammon,
and Arabah as far as sea of Chinneroth eastward, and as far as sea of Arabah, Salt Sea, eastward by way of Beth-jeshimoth, and southward under slopes of Pisgah.
And territory of Og king of Bashan, from remnant of Rephaim, dwelling at Ashtaroth and at Edrei,
and ruling at Mount Hermon, Salecah, and all Bashan as far as border of Geshurites and Maacathites, and half Gilead, border of Sihon king of Heshbon.
Moses servant of YHWH and children of Israel struck them, and Moses servant of YHWH gave it as possession to Reubenites, Gadites, and half tribe of Manasseh.

These are kings of land whom Joshua and children of Israel struck across Jordan westward, from Baal-gad in valley of Lebanon as far as Mount Halak rising toward Seir; Joshua gave it to tribes of Israel as possession according to their divisions,
in hill country, Shephelah, Arabah, slopes, wilderness, and Negev: Hittite, Amorite, Canaanite, Perizzite, Hivite, and Jebusite:
king of Jericho, one; king of Ai, which is beside Bethel, one;
king of Jerusalem, one; king of Hebron, one;
king of Jarmuth, one; king of Lachish, one;
king of Eglon, one; king of Gezer, one;
king of Debir, one; king of Geder, one;
king of Hormah, one; king of Arad, one;
king of Libnah, one; king of Adullam, one;
king of Makkedah, one; king of Bethel, one;
king of Tappuah, one; king of Hepher, one;
king of Aphek, one; king of Lasharon, one;
king of Madon, one; king of Hazor, one;
king of Shimron-meron, one; king of Achshaph, one;
king of Taanach, one; king of Megiddo, one;
king of Kedesh, one; king of Jokneam in Carmel, one;
king of Dor in heights of Dor, one; king of Goiim in Gilgal, one;
king of Tirzah, one. All kings: thirty-one.

translation_notes: The chapter is an administrative victory list, not a fresh narrative. It compresses battles, cities, civilians, and political communities into named kings and counted outcomes.
The east-Jordan section links Moses' campaigns to Joshua's western campaigns, reinforcing succession and territorial continuity.
Several place identifications remain uncertain, including Lasharon and Goiim in Gilgal; textual witnesses and historical geography require audit.
The thirty-one total may function as a literary conquest summary and should not be treated as a simple archaeological inventory.
Some kings in the list are not individually narrated in chapters 6-11, suggesting condensed traditions, broader campaign memory, or compositional layering.
Counting rulers as defeated can make the dead population disappear from view. The list must be read alongside the no-survivor formulas that precede it.
The territorial register is ancient literary geography and cannot serve as a modern deed or border claim.
safety_note: Do not use Joshua 12 as a modern conquest ledger, territorial deed, nationalist scorecard, or proof that violence establishes moral ownership. Read the list critically as a victor's administrative memory that leaves civilians, losses, and displaced communities largely unnamed.
